title: Crisp sandwich
subtitle: Buttered white bread filled with ready salted crisps
excerpt: The crisp sandwich is exactly what it sounds like. Buttered white bread, a generous handful of ready salted crisps, pressed together so the crisps crunch. It is not a recipe so much as a known quantity, and the British have been eating them for decades.
sourceNotes: British snack food, widespread across the country. The crisp sandwich has been eaten since at least the 1960s and is particularly associated with school lunchboxes and after-school snacks. It requires no cooking and almost no assembly time.

content.text: The crisp sandwich has one rule: the crisps must be inside the bread, not beside it, and they must crunch when you bite. The flavour of crisp is a matter of personal conviction. Ready salted is the standard. Salt and vinegar is the alternative. Cheese and onion is also strongly represented. Whatever your choice: white bread, real butter, and enough crisps to fill the middle completely.

items.prepNote: ready salted or your preferred flavour

content.text: Butter both slices of bread all the way to the edges.
Open the crisp packet and tip the crisps onto one slice of bread in an even layer. Cover completely from edge to edge.
Place the second slice of bread on top, buttered side down. Press firmly so the crisps break slightly and hold together. Eat immediately before they soften.

items.cause: The sandwich was assembled too far ahead.
items.fix: Eat the crisp sandwich within 2 minutes of making it. The crunch is the whole point.

items.symptom: The crisps slide out when you bite
items.cause: Not enough pressure when assembling, or too few crisps.
items.fix: Press firmly when you close the sandwich. The butter holds the crisps to the bread if applied to both sides.

items.cause: Unsalted crisps, or not enough butter.
items.fix: Use well-seasoned crisps and do not stint on the butter.

content.text: The crisp sandwich is widely eaten but rarely discussed. Almost everyone who eats one feels slightly as though they should not. In reality it is salt, fat, carbohydrate, and crunch in a useful format. The British have been quietly eating them since crisps became affordable in the 1960s. There is nothing wrong with it.
